claims.statement: The method provides a full root shell on the Carlinkit CPC200-CCPA / A15W adapter's Linux rootfs over the USB cable only, with no Wi-Fi association required.
claims.quote: a full root shell on the adapter's Linux rootfs over the **USB cable only** — no Wi‑Fi association required

claims.statement: The adapter exposes a USB CDC-NCM ('USB Ethernet') gadget; the host gets a network interface on the same link, and the adapter's dropbear SSH daemon (listening on all interfaces) is reachable over it.
claims.quote: The adapter exposes a USB CDC‑NCM ("USB Ethernet") gadget; your host gets a network interface on the same link, and the adapter's `dropbear` SSH daemon (already listening on all interfaces) is reachable over it.

claims.statement: Verified live on 2026-05-30 with firmware build 2025.09.26.
claims.quote: Verified live on 2026‑05‑30: firmware build `2025.09.26`

claims.statement: The kernel is 3.14.52+g94d07bb (armv7l architecture).
claims.quote: kernel `3.14.52+g94d07bb` (armv7l)

claims.statement: The adapter hostname is sk_mainboard.
claims.quote: hostname `sk_mainboard`

claims.statement: The verification host was macOS (Apple Silicon); the same approach works on Linux; Windows needs a CDC-NCM driver.
claims.quote: Host: macOS (Apple Silicon). The same approach works on Linux; Windows needs a CDC‑NCM driver (see notes).

claims.statement: After arming, you connect from the host with: ssh root@192.168.50.2.
claims.quote: On the adapter (one-time per boot), then from the host:  ssh root@192.168.50.2

claims.statement: Everything in the procedure is volatile (RAM/sysfs/tmp); a reboot restores normal CarPlay/Android Auto operation.
claims.quote: Everything is volatile (RAM/sysfs/tmp) — a **reboot restores normal CarPlay/Android Auto operation**.

claims.statement: The adapter has two USB device controllers (ChipIdea) and two Android-gadget instances.
claims.quote: The adapter has **two USB device controllers** (ChipIdea) and **two Android‑gadget instances**

claims.statement: The sysfs gadget class /sys/class/android_usb_accessory/android0 maps to controller ci_hdrc.1 and is the external port (the plug that goes into the car/host); its default state is AOA 'accessory' gadget with VID 1314 PID 1521 ('Auto Box').
claims.quote: `/sys/class/android_usb_accessory/android0` | `ci_hdrc.1` | **external** port (the plug that goes into the car / your host) | AOA "accessory" gadget, VID `1314` PID `1521` ("Auto Box")

claims.statement: The sysfs gadget class /sys/class/android_usb/android0 maps to controller ci_hdrc.0 and is the phone-side port; its default state is an iap2,ncm composite with VID 08e4 PID 01c0 (only active with a phone).
claims.quote: `/sys/class/android_usb/android0` | `ci_hdrc.0` | phone‑side port | `iap2,ncm` composite, VID `08e4` PID `01c0` (only active with a phone)

claims.statement: A userspace daemon ARMadb-driver is started by /script/start_main_service.sh, which spawns /script/phone_link_deamon.sh CarPlay start.
claims.quote: A userspace daemon, **`ARMadb-driver`** (started by `/script/start_main_service.sh`, which spawns `/script/phone_link_deamon.sh CarPlay start`)

claims.statement: ARMadb-driver continuously cycles the external gadget looking for a CarPlay head unit or Android Auto host; a generic computer is neither, so the port stays in accessory/AOA mode and the host never sees an Ethernet interface.
claims.quote: continuously **cycles the external gadget** looking for a CarPlay head unit or an Android Auto host. A generic computer is neither, so the port stays in accessory/AOA mode and your host never sees an Ethernet interface.

claims.statement: The f_ncm function binds cleanly on either gadget.
claims.quote: the `f_ncm` function binds cleanly on either gadget

claims.statement: /script/start_ncm.sh assigns ncm0 = 192.168.50.2/24 at boot.
claims.quote: `/script/start_ncm.sh` already assigns **`ncm0 = 192.168.50.2/24`** at boot

claims.statement: dropbear listens on 0.0.0.0:22 and :::22, so it answers on ncm0 too.
claims.quote: `dropbear` listens on `0.0.0.0:22` **and** `:::22`, so it answers on `ncm0` too

claims.statement: The overall task is to stop the cycler, switch the external gadget to NCM, and hand the host an IP.
claims.quote: **stop the cycler, switch the external gadget to NCM, hand the host an IP.**

claims.statement: A control channel to the adapter is needed to issue setup commands the first time; easiest is the adapter's own Wi-Fi AP.
claims.quote: A **control channel to the adapter** to issue the setup commands the first time. Easiest is the adapter's own Wi‑Fi AP

claims.statement: SSH to the adapter over Wi-Fi is at root@192.168.43.1 with a blank password (just press Enter).
claims.quote: SSH: `ssh root@192.168.43.1` (password is **blank** — just press Enter)

claims.statement: On macOS, non-interactive Wi-Fi SSH login uses: sshpass -p '' ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no root@192.168.43.1.
claims.quote: On macOS, non‑interactive: `sshpass -p '' ssh -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no root@192.168.43.1`

claims.statement: Alternatively a serial/UART console can be used as the control channel.
claims.quote: Or a serial/UART console if you have one.

claims.statement: The adapter must be connected to the host via the USB data cable (the normal connector that plugs into the car).
claims.quote: The adapter connected to your host via the **USB data cable** (the normal connector that plugs into the car).

claims.statement: sshpass is optional but handy for the blank password; install on macOS via brew install hudochenkov/sshpass/sshpass.
claims.quote: `sshpass` is optional but handy for the blank password (`brew install hudochenkov/sshpass/sshpass`)

claims.statement: The Wi-Fi control channel is only needed once to arm NCM; after that the USB link stands on its own.
claims.quote: You only need the Wi‑Fi control channel **once** to arm NCM. After that the USB link stands on its own.

claims.statement: The arming commands are safe and volatile.
claims.quote: They are safe and volatile.

claims.statement: The gadget cycler must be stopped or it reverts NCM within ~2 seconds; ARMadb-driver is NOT auto-respawned, so killing it makes NCM stick.
claims.quote:
Stop the gadget cycler (otherwise it reverts NCM within ~2 s).
#      ARMadb-driver is NOT auto-respawned, so killing it makes NCM stick.

claims.statement: To stop the cycler, run: killall ARMadb-driver ARMiPhoneIAP2 fakeiOSDevice 2>/dev/null and pkill -f phone_link_deamon 2>/dev/null.
claims.quote:
killall ARMadb-driver ARMiPhoneIAP2 fakeiOSDevice 2>/dev/null
pkill -f phone_link_deamon 2>/dev/null

claims.statement: The phone-side composite that owns the ncm0 netdev is released with: echo 0 > /sys/class/android_usb/android0/enable.
claims.quote:
Release the phone-side composite that owns the ncm0 netdev.
echo 0 > /sys/class/android_usb/android0/enable

claims.statement: To switch the external gadget to pure CDC-NCM, set bDeviceClass=239 (0xEF Miscellaneous IAD), bDeviceSubClass=2, bDeviceProtocol=1, functions=ncm, after disabling and then re-enabling via $A/enable, where A=/sys/class/android_usb_accessory/android0.
claims.quote:
A=/sys/class/android_usb_accessory/android0
echo 0   > $A/enable
echo 239 > $A/bDeviceClass      # 0xEF Miscellaneous (IAD)
echo 2   > $A/bDeviceSubClass
echo 1   > $A/bDeviceProtocol
echo ncm > $A/functions
echo 1   > $A/enable

claims.statement: The netdev address is set with: busybox ifconfig ncm0 192.168.50.2 netmask 255.255.255.0 mtu 1500 up.
claims.quote: busybox ifconfig ncm0 192.168.50.2 netmask 255.255.255.0 mtu 1500 up

claims.statement: Verification: cat /sys/class/android_usb_accessory/android0/functions should return 'ncm'.
claims.quote: cat /sys/class/android_usb_accessory/android0/functions   # -> ncm

claims.statement: Verification: cat /sys/class/android_usb_accessory/android0/state should return 'CONFIGURED'.
claims.quote: cat /sys/class/android_usb_accessory/android0/state        # -> CONFIGURED

claims.statement: Verification: cat /sys/class/udc/ci_hdrc.1/state should return 'configured'.
claims.quote: cat /sys/class/udc/ci_hdrc.1/state                         # -> configured

claims.statement: dmesg should show: android_enable_function: ncm, ncm_function_bind_config MAC: …, and ncm0: HOST MAC <xx>; that HOST MAC is the MAC the host uses for its new interface.
claims.quote: `dmesg` should show: `android_enable_function: ncm`, `ncm_function_bind_config MAC: …`, `ncm0: HOST MAC <xx>`. That `HOST MAC` is the MAC your host will use for its new interface.

claims.statement: By default /etc/udhcpd.conf serves only wlan0 (the 'interface ncm0' line is commented out).
claims.quote: By default `/etc/udhcpd.conf` serves **only `wlan0`** (the `interface ncm0` line is commented out)

claims.statement: To serve DHCP on ncm0 without disturbing the Wi-Fi one, start a second independent udhcpd bound to ncm0 using a /tmp/udhcpd_ncm.conf with start 192.168.50.100, end 192.168.50.150, interface ncm0, router 192.168.50.2, subnet 255.255.255.0, dns 192.168.50.2, lease 86400, lease_file /tmp/udhcpd_ncm.leases, pidfile /tmp/udhcpd_ncm.pid, max_leases 20.
claims.quote:
start       192.168.50.100
end         192.168.50.150
interface   ncm0
opt router  192.168.50.2
opt subnet  255.255.255.0
opt dns     192.168.50.2
opt lease   86400
lease_file  /tmp/udhcpd_ncm.leases
pidfile     /tmp/udhcpd_ncm.pid
max_leases  20

claims.statement: The DHCP server is started with: setsid busybox udhcpd -f /tmp/udhcpd_ncm.conf after touching /tmp/udhcpd_ncm.leases.
claims.quote:
touch /tmp/udhcpd_ncm.leases
setsid busybox udhcpd -f /tmp/udhcpd_ncm.conf >/tmp/udhcpd_ncm.log 2>&1 </dev/null &

claims.statement: Without DHCP you can still connect via IPv6 link-local.
claims.quote: (Without this you can still connect via IPv6 link‑local — see §4b.)

claims.statement: Bouncing the gadget once (echo 0 > $A/enable; sleep 2; echo 1 > $A/enable) makes the host tear down and re-create the interface, triggering a fresh DHCP request that picks up the lease immediately.
claims.quote: bouncing the gadget once (`echo 0 > $A/enable; sleep 2; echo 1 > $A/enable`) makes the host tear down and re‑create the interface, which triggers a fresh DHCP request and picks up the lease immediately

claims.statement: When the NCM gadget enumerates, macOS and modern Linux create a new Ethernet interface automatically with no driver install (both have a built-in CDC-NCM driver).
claims.quote: no driver install on macOS or modern Linux — both have a built‑in CDC‑NCM driver

claims.statement: The new host interface's MAC equals the adapter's 'HOST MAC' from dmesg; on macOS list interfaces with ifconfig -l / ifconfig enX, on Linux with ip -br link.
claims.quote:
Find the new interface: its MAC equals the adapter's "HOST MAC" from dmesg.
#   macOS: ifconfig -l ; ifconfig enX        Linux: ip -br link

claims.statement: With the §3.5 DHCP running the host gets an address in 192.168.50.100-150 automatically, then ssh root@192.168.50.2 (blank password).
claims.quote:
With §3.5 DHCP running it gets 192.168.50.100-150 automatically:
ssh root@192.168.50.2          # password: blank

claims.statement: To confirm the connection went over USB not Wi-Fi: route -n get 192.168.50.2 | grep interface (macOS, should show the USB enX), and inside the session echo $SSH_CONNECTION should read 192.168.50.100 … 192.168.50.2 22.
claims.quote:
route -n get 192.168.50.2 | grep interface   # macOS -> the USB enX
# inside the session:  echo $SSH_CONNECTION   -> 192.168.50.100 … 192.168.50.2 22

claims.statement: dropbear listens on IPv6 too, and IPv6 link-local needs no address config (no DHCP, no admin rights).
claims.quote: `dropbear` listens on IPv6 too, and link‑local needs no address config

claims.statement: The adapter's ncm0 IPv6 link-local address is derived from its MAC, e.g. fe80::c08e:30ff:fe52:e9a3; connect with ssh -6 root@fe80::c08e:30ff:fe52:e9a3%en7 where the host scope is the new enX (e.g. en7 on macOS, enpXsY on Linux).
claims.quote:
adapter ncm0 IPv6-LL is derived from its MAC, e.g. fe80::c08e:30ff:fe52:e9a3
# host interface scope is the new enX (e.g. en7 on macOS, enpXsY on Linux)
ssh -6 root@fe80::c08e:30ff:fe52:e9a3%en7

claims.statement: The adapter's IPv6-LL value can be obtained with busybox ifconfig ncm0 | grep inet6 over the control channel.
claims.quote: Get the adapter's value with `busybox ifconfig ncm0 | grep inet6` over your control channel.

claims.statement: Static IPv4 on macOS: sudo ifconfig en7 192.168.50.1 netmask 255.255.255.0 up; on Linux: sudo ip addr add 192.168.50.1/24 dev enpXsY && sudo ip link set enpXsY up; then ssh root@192.168.50.2.
claims.quote:
sudo ifconfig en7 192.168.50.1 netmask 255.255.255.0 up   # macOS
sudo ip addr add 192.168.50.1/24 dev enpXsY && sudo ip link set enpXsY up  # Linux
ssh root@192.168.50.2

claims.statement: Windows doesn't ship a generic CDC-NCM class driver; either install a CDC-NCM/usb_ncm INF or have the adapter present RNDIS instead (echo rndis > $A/functions) which Windows binds natively.
claims.quote: Windows doesn't ship a generic CDC‑NCM class driver. Either install a CDC‑NCM/usb_ncm INF, or have the adapter present **RNDIS** instead (`echo rndis > $A/functions`) which Windows binds natively.

claims.statement: With RNDIS the adapter appears as a 'USB Ethernet/RNDIS Gadget' NIC on Windows; use DHCP or static 192.168.50.1.
claims.quote: the adapter appears as a "USB Ethernet/RNDIS Gadget" NIC; use DHCP (§3.5) or static `192.168.50.1`

claims.statement: macOS does not support RNDIS; keep ncm for Apple hosts.
claims.quote: macOS does **not** support RNDIS — keep `ncm` for Apple hosts.

claims.statement: To restore normal operation, run reboot on the adapter; boot re-runs start_main_service / start_iap2_ncm / start_ncm.
claims.quote: reboot          # on the adapter — boot re-runs start_main_service / start_iap2_ncm / start_ncm

claims.statement: There is no clean 'undo' short of reboot, because killing ARMadb-driver tears down the projection state machine.
claims.quote: There is no clean "undo" short of reboot, because killing `ARMadb-driver` tears down the projection state machine.

claims.statement: start_main_service.sh runs 'test -e /script/custom_init.sh && /script/custom_init.sh' early in boot; a custom_init.sh could arm a management NCM link automatically.
claims.quote: `start_main_service.sh` runs `test -e /script/custom_init.sh && /script/custom_init.sh` early in boot. A `custom_init.sh` could arm a management NCM link automatically.

claims.statement: The external port ci_hdrc.1 is single-gadget: a dedicated management-NCM conflicts with the CarPlay/AOA gadget on that same port; both cannot run simultaneously unless NCM is composited into the projection gadget (e.g. iap2,ncm or rndis,ncm multifunction) or NCM is only armed when not actively projecting.
claims.quote: The external port `ci_hdrc.1` is single‑gadget: a dedicated management‑NCM **conflicts** with the CarPlay/AOA gadget the box wants on that same port. You can't have both simultaneously unless NCM is **composited into** the projection gadget

claims.statement: /script may live on a read-only rootfs; persisting a file there can require remounting rw or repacking firmware, and the A15W image is AES-encrypted.
claims.quote: `/script` may live on a read‑only rootfs; persisting a file there can require remounting rw or repacking firmware (the A15W image is AES‑encrypted — see your firmware tooling).

claims.statement: During a normal wired-CarPlay session against a real head unit, the box brings up iap2,ncm and ncm0 carries the CarPlay IPv6 session; because dropbear listens on ncm0, SSH over USB is reachable from the head-unit side with no modification and no Wi-Fi, the head unit being the NCM peer.
claims.quote: during a **normal wired‑CarPlay session against a real head unit**, the box already brings up `iap2,ncm` and `ncm0` carries the CarPlay IPv6 session. Because `dropbear` is listening on `ncm0`, SSH over USB is reachable **from the head‑unit side with no modification and no Wi‑Fi at all**

claims.statement: Troubleshooting: NCM flips back to 'Auto Box' after ~2 s when ARMadb-driver is still running; fix with killall ARMadb-driver and pkill -f phone_link_deamon; it is not auto-respawned.
claims.quote: NCM flips back to "Auto Box" after ~2 s | `ARMadb-driver` still running — `killall ARMadb-driver` (and `pkill -f phone_link_deamon`). It is **not** auto‑respawned.

claims.statement: Troubleshooting: if echo 1 > .../enable reads back 0, the other gadget still holds ncm0; run echo 0 > /sys/class/android_usb/android0/enable first.
claims.quote: `echo 1 > .../enable` reads back `0` | The other gadget still holds `ncm0`. Run `echo 0 > /sys/class/android_usb/android0/enable` first (§3.2).

claims.statement: Troubleshooting: if the host interface appears but stays on 169.254.x / no IP, there is no DHCP on ncm0; start udhcpd and bounce the gadget, or use IPv6-LL / static IPv4.
claims.quote: Host interface appears but stays on `169.254.x` / no IP | No DHCP on `ncm0`. Start udhcpd (§3.5) and bounce the gadget

claims.statement: Troubleshooting: if no new interface appears on the host, check state is CONFIGURED and ci_hdrc.1 state is configured, replug the USB cable, and on Windows use RNDIS.
claims.quote: No new interface on the host at all | Check `cat .../state` is `CONFIGURED` and `cat /sys/class/udc/ci_hdrc.1/state` is `configured`; replug the USB cable; on Windows use RNDIS (§4d).

claims.statement: Troubleshooting: 'busybox udhcpd: not found' occurs because it's a busybox applet; call it as 'busybox udhcpd …', not 'udhcpd'.
claims.quote: `busybox udhcpd: not found` | It's a busybox applet — call it as `busybox udhcpd …`, not `udhcpd`.

claims.statement: The .50.x subnet only exists on the USB-NCM link, so ssh root@192.168.50.2 and checking echo $SSH_CONNECTION proves the connection is over USB.
claims.quote: `ssh root@192.168.50.2` (the `.50.x` subnet only exists on the USB‑NCM link) and check `echo $SSH_CONNECTION`.

claims.statement: A one-shot helper script can be saved as /tmp/ncm_ssh.sh and run with sh /tmp/ncm_ssh.sh; it includes a re-DHCP nudge (echo 0 > $A/enable; sleep 2; echo ncm > $A/functions; echo 1 > $A/enable) and prints the resulting state.
claims.quote: Save as `/tmp/ncm_ssh.sh`, then `sh /tmp/ncm_ssh.sh`

claims.statement: After arming, connecting from the host is done with ssh root@192.168.50.2 using a blank password, giving a root shell over USB without Wi-Fi.
claims.quote: on the host: **`ssh root@192.168.50.2`** (blank password). Done — root shell over USB, no Wi‑Fi.

claims.statement: Command 500 (0x1F4) is RequestVideoFocus, A→H, adapter requests host display video
claims.quote: | 500 | RequestVideoFocus | A→H | Adapter requests host display video |

claims.statement: The firmware binaries analyzed are located at /Users/zeno/Downloads/misc/cpc200_ccpa_firmware_binaries/old_firmware/.
claims.quote: Firmware binaries: `/Users/zeno/Downloads/misc/cpc200_ccpa_firmware_binaries/old_firmware/`

claims.statement: The adapter model is the Carlinkit CPC200-CCPA (A15W), a wireless CarPlay/Android Auto adapter.
claims.quote: **Model:** Carlinkit CPC200-CCPA (A15W) Wireless CarPlay/Android Auto Adapter

claims.statement: The documentation was consolidated on 2026-01-16.
claims.quote: **Consolidated:** 2026-01-16

claims.statement: Sources for the documentation are firmware extraction, session captures, and live reverse engineering.
claims.quote: **Sources:** Firmware extraction, session captures, and live RE

claims.statement: The evidence base comprises 25+ controlled capture sessions, deep r2 binary analysis of 7 main executables plus 10 shared libraries, and 40+ analysis documents.
claims.quote: **Evidence Base:** 25+ controlled capture sessions, deep r2 binary analysis of 7 main executables + 10 shared libraries, 40+ analysis documents

claims.statement: The CPC200-CCPA is a protocol bridge, not a media endpoint.
claims.quote: The CPC200-CCPA is a **protocol bridge**, not a media endpoint.

claims.statement: In the data flow, the iPhone/Android (CarPlay UI, H.264 encoded) connects via AirPlay/iAP2/AA to the adapter (TUNNEL/forward), which connects via USB to the Host App (DECODER/policy).
claims.quote: iPhone/Android                    Adapter                         Host App

claims.statement: The adapter performs protocol handshake and authentication.
claims.quote: - Protocol handshake and authentication

claims.statement: The adapter performs session establishment with the phone.
claims.quote: - Session establishment with phone

claims.statement: The adapter forwards H.264 video verbatim with no decode and no transcode.
claims.quote: - Forward H.264 video verbatim (no decode, no transcode)

claims.statement: The adapter forwards PCM audio verbatim in the playback direction.
claims.quote: - Forward PCM audio verbatim (playback direction)

claims.statement: The adapter processes microphone audio using WebRTC AGC, AECM echo cancellation, and NS noise suppression.
claims.quote: - Process microphone audio (WebRTC AGC, AECM echo cancellation, NS noise suppression)

claims.statement: The adapter prepends USB headers to data streams.
claims.quote: - Prepend USB headers to data streams

claims.statement: The adapter responds to keyframe requests.
claims.quote: - Respond to keyframe requests

claims.statement: The adapter does NOT buffer video for quality.
claims.quote: - Buffer video for quality

claims.statement: The adapter does NOT pace or smooth frames.
claims.quote: - Pace or smooth frames

claims.statement: The adapter does NOT correct timing or sync.
claims.quote: - Correct timing or sync

claims.statement: The adapter does NOT reset the decoder on errors.
claims.quote: - Reset decoder on errors

claims.statement: The adapter does NOT compensate for jitter.
claims.quote: - Compensate for jitter

claims.statement: The adapter does NOT make video policy decisions.
claims.quote: - Make video policy decisions

claims.statement: All correctness decisions are the host application's responsibility.
claims.quote: **All correctness decisions are the host application's responsibility.**

claims.statement: The adapter provides raw H.264 NAL units; the host must handle H.264 decoding.
claims.quote: | Raw H.264 NAL units | H.264 decoding |

claims.statement: The adapter provides USB frame headers with PTS; the host must handle timing policy (or ignore PTS).
claims.quote: | USB frame headers with PTS | Timing policy (or ignore PTS) |

claims.statement: The adapter provides passthrough without buffering; the host must handle jitter absorption.
claims.quote: | Passthrough without buffering | Jitter absorption |

claims.statement: The adapter provides keyframe on request (100-200ms); the host must handle error detection and recovery.
claims.quote: | Keyframe on request (100-200ms) | Error detection and recovery |

claims.statement: The adapter provides no quality assessment; the host must handle corruption detection and reset.
claims.quote: | No quality assessment | Corruption detection and reset |

claims.statement: The adapter is neutral; if video corrupts, the host app's policy is wrong.
claims.quote: **The adapter is neutral. If video corrupts, the host app's policy is wrong.**

claims.statement: The processor is an NXP i.MX6UL (ARM Cortex-A7, 32-bit).
claims.quote: | **Processor** | NXP i.MX6UL (ARM Cortex-A7, 32-bit) |

claims.statement: The adapter has 128MB of RAM.
The adapter has 16MB of Flash storage.
claims.quote: | **Storage** | 16MB Flash |

claims.statement: The WiFi chip is a Realtek RTL88x2CS (5GHz 802.11ac); hardware revisions are known to use others.
claims.quote: | **WiFi** | Realtek RTL88x2CS (5GHz 802.11ac); hardware revisions are known to use others |

claims.statement: The Bluetooth is a Realtek HCI UART supporting BR/EDR and BLE.
claims.quote: | **Bluetooth** | Realtek HCI UART (BR/EDR + BLE) |

claims.statement: The USB protocol uses magic 0x55AA55AA, a 16-byte header size, and a maximum payload of 1MB.
claims.quote: | 0x55AA55AA | 16 bytes | 1MB |

claims.statement: Message type 1 (0x01) is Open: session init with a 28-byte 7×uint32-LE payload [width,height,fps,format,packetMax,boxVersion,phoneMode]; handler ProceessCmdOpen.
claims.quote: Session init — 28-byte 7×uint32-LE payload `[width,height,fps,format,packetMax,boxVersion,phoneMode]`; handler `ProceessCmdOpen`

claims.statement: Message type 6 (0x06) is VideoData: H.264 main video frames.
claims.quote: | 6 | 0x06 | VideoData | H.264 main video frames |

claims.statement: Message type 7 (0x07) is AudioData: PCM audio or commands (1-byte cmd).
claims.quote: | 7 | 0x07 | AudioData | PCM audio or commands (1-byte cmd) |

claims.statement: Message type 8 (0x08) is Command: control commands, bidirectional — host→adapter (e.g. 12 keyFrame, 508 nav-screen focus) AND adapter→host (e.g. 18/19 GNSS start/stop, 506/507 audio nav focus), including on wired CarPlay.
claims.quote: **bidirectional**: host→adapter (e.g. 12 keyFrame, 508 nav-screen focus) AND adapter→host (e.g. 18/19 GNSS start/stop, 506/507 audio nav focus), incl. on wired CarPlay

claims.statement: Message type 23 (0x17) is MultiTouch: touch input host→adapter with 16-byte payload [float x(0..1), float y(0..1), uint32 action(0=up,1=down,2=move), uint32 fingerId].
claims.quote: Touch input host→adapter — 16-byte `[float x(0..1), float y(0..1), uint32 action(0=up,1=down,2=move), uint32 fingerId]`

claims.statement: Message type 25 (0x19) is BoxSettings: JSON config parsed by ARMadb-driver FUN_00016c20, with a 29-entry map at 0x93f90.
claims.quote: JSON config — parsed by ARMadb-driver `FUN_00016c20`, 29-entry map at `0x93f90`

claims.statement: Message type 41 (0x29) is GnssData: GPS/GNSS to phone (NMEA).
claims.quote: | 41 | 0x29 | GnssData | GPS/GNSS to phone (NMEA) |

claims.statement: Message type 42 (0x2A) is MetaData: plaintext JSON telemetry adapter→host, with a leading uint32 subtype and null-terminated JSON; subtype 1=Media (MediaSongPlayTime, MediaPlayStatus), subtype 200=Navi turn-by-turn (NaviStatus, NaviTimeToDestination, NaviDestinationName, NaviRemainDistance, NaviRoadName).
claims.quote: Plaintext JSON telemetry adapter→host — leading uint32 subtype + null-terminated JSON. Subtype 1=Media (`MediaSongPlayTime`,`MediaPlayStatus`), 200=Navi turn-by-turn

claims.statement: Message type 44 (0x2C) is NaviVideoData: secondary navigation / instrument-cluster H.264 stream (CarPlay, iOS 13+), using the same 36-byte header as 0x06.
claims.quote: Secondary navigation / instrument-cluster H.264 stream (CarPlay, iOS 13+). Same 36-byte header as 0x06.

claims.statement: Message type 153 (0x99) is SendFile: flat-file plus HU_* struct upload, staging at /tmp/uploadFileTmp, runs as root.
claims.quote: Flat-file + `HU_*` struct upload (staging `/tmp/uploadFileTmp`, runs as root)

claims.statement: Message type 170 (0xAA) is HeartBeat: keep-alive at 2s interval, host→adapter, with 0-byte payload.
claims.quote: Keep-alive (2s interval), host→adapter, 0-byte payload

claims.statement: The 0x17, 0x2A, and 0x2C message-type rows were added 2026-06-14 from macOS-host captures; they were live in every session but missing from the master table.
claims.quote: The 0x17 / 0x2A / 0x2C rows above were added 2026-06-14 from macOS-host captures (they were live in every session but missing from this master table).

claims.statement: Doc provenance note: entries span both the carlink_macOS (macOS host) and carlink_native (Android host) apps plus multiple adapter firmware revisions; some quirks/comments may be host- or firmware-specific and may no longer apply.
claims.quote: Some quirks/comments are host- or firmware-specific and may no longer apply. Prefer dated "Verified findings" blocks; treat undated claims with caution.

claims.statement: decode_type 2 is 44.1kHz Stereo and is dual-purpose: Commands (13 bytes) OR 44.1kHz audio (non-default, only when mediaSound=0); status VERIFIED.
claims.quote: **Dual-purpose:** Commands (13 bytes) OR 44.1kHz audio (non-default, only when `mediaSound=0`)

claims.statement: decode_type 3 is 8kHz Mono for phone call (narrowband), active for Android Auto phone calls (8kHz narrowband via HFP/SCO), not used by CarPlay (which uses 16kHz).
claims.quote: Active for Android Auto phone calls (8kHz narrowband via HFP/SCO). Not used by CarPlay (uses 16kHz).

claims.statement: decode_type 4 is 48kHz Stereo, the standard media audio default for both CarPlay and Android Auto; status VERIFIED.
claims.quote: Standard media audio (default for both CarPlay and Android Auto)

claims.statement: decode_type 5 is 16kHz Mono for voice (Siri, phone calls); status VERIFIED.
claims.quote: | 5 | 16kHz Mono | Voice (Siri, phone calls) | VERIFIED |

claims.statement: The adapter is transparent and passes through whatever sample rate the phone negotiates; CarPlay and AA both default to 48kHz (decodeType=4), and 44.1kHz (decodeType=2) only appears when explicitly configured via mediaSound=0.
claims.quote: The adapter is transparent — it passes through whatever sample rate the phone negotiates. CarPlay and AA both default to 48kHz (decodeType=4). 44.1kHz (decodeType=2) only appears when explicitly configured via `mediaSound=0`.

claims.statement: decode_type=2 behavior depends on payload size.
claims.quote: decode_type=2 behavior depends on payload size

claims.statement: The CallQuality Web UI setting has a firmware bug and does not affect the sample rate.
claims.quote: CallQuality Web UI setting has a **firmware bug** and does not affect sample rate

claims.statement: audio_type 1 is Main channel, Playback direction.
claims.quote: | 1 | Main | Playback |

claims.statement: audio_type 2 is Navigation channel, Playback direction (ducking).
claims.quote: | 2 | Navigation | Playback (ducking) |

claims.statement: audio_type 3 is Microphone channel, Capture direction.
claims.quote: | 3 | Microphone | Capture |

claims.statement: Heartbeat must start BEFORE sending initialization messages.
claims.quote: Heartbeat must start **BEFORE** sending initialization messages:

claims.statement: The initialization sequence is: USB Reset, 3-second wait, Open USB connection, START HEARTBEAT IMMEDIATELY, Send initialization messages, Start reading loop.
claims.quote:
1. USB Reset
2. 3-second wait
3. Open USB connection
4. START HEARTBEAT IMMEDIATELY  ← Critical
5. Send initialization messages
6. Start reading loop

claims.statement: Failure to start heartbeat before initialization causes cold start failures, taking about 11.7 seconds to disconnect.
claims.quote: Failure to do this causes cold start failures (~11.7 seconds to disconnect).

claims.statement: There are 15 verified audio commands in the range 0x01-0x10.
claims.quote: 15 verified audio commands (0x01-0x10):

claims.statement: No SIRI_STOP command exists; sessions end via OUTPUT_STOP.
claims.quote: **No SIRI_STOP exists** - sessions end via OUTPUT_STOP

claims.statement: NAVI_STOP (0x08) activates Siri mode (a misleading name).
claims.quote: **NAVI_STOP (0x08)** activates Siri mode (misleading name)

claims.statement: NAVI_COMPLETE (0x10) signals navigation prompt finished.
claims.quote: **NAVI_COMPLETE (0x10)** signals navigation prompt finished

claims.statement: To request an IDR keyframe, send the Frame command (0x0C); the adapter responds within 100-200ms with SPS + PPS + IDR frame.
claims.quote: Send Frame command (0x0C) to request IDR. Adapter responds within 100-200ms with SPS + PPS + IDR frame.

claims.statement: Video session start: 100% begin with SPS+PPS+IDR, across 10/10 sessions.
claims.quote: | Session start | 100% begin with SPS+PPS+IDR | 10/10 sessions |

claims.statement: SPS/PPS bundling: always with IDR, never standalone, across 538+ IDRs.
claims.quote: | SPS/PPS bundling | Always with IDR, never standalone | 538+ IDRs |

claims.statement: IDR interval: median 2000ms (range 83-2117ms), from 538 intervals.
claims.quote: | IDR interval | Median 2000ms (range 83-2117ms) | 538 intervals |

claims.statement: Jitter standard deviation is 25.6ms (85% within ±40ms), from 215K frames.
claims.quote: | Jitter std dev | 25.6ms (85% within ±40ms) | 215K frames |

claims.statement: Frame sizes: IDR averages 49KB, P-frames average 23KB, from 215K frames.
claims.quote: | Frame sizes | IDR: 49KB avg, P: 23KB avg | 215K frames |

claims.statement: Change log 2026-06-13: Android Auto ViewArea/SafeArea binary-confirmed via radare2 — ARMAndroidAuto's embedded VideoConfigData.proto descriptor (aasdk openauto-v2 SDK) defines VideoConfig with only video_resolution, video_fps, margin_width, margin_height, density, additional_depth, real_density — no ui_config, no content_insets, no Insets.
claims.quote: `ARMAndroidAuto`'s embedded `VideoConfigData.proto` descriptor (aasdk **openauto-v2** SDK) defines `VideoConfig` with only `video_resolution, video_fps, margin_width, margin_height, density, additional_depth, real_density` — **no `ui_config`, no `content_insets`, no `Insets`**

claims.statement: Android Auto can emit only a symmetric letterbox margin; a content-inset SafeArea is structurally impossible.
claims.quote: So AA can emit only a symmetric letterbox margin; a content-inset SafeArea is *structurally impossible*.

claims.statement: The AA view rect is sized from androidAutoSizeW/H keys, not HU_VIEWAREA_INFO (whose only ARMadb-driver xref is ProceessCmdOpen, the CarPlay/Open handler).
claims.quote: AA view rect is sized from `androidAutoSizeW/H` keys, **not** `HU_VIEWAREA_INFO` (whose only `ARMadb-driver` xref is `ProceessCmdOpen`, the CarPlay/Open handler)

claims.statement: Bare HU_SAFEAREA_INFO is read only by AppleCarPlay, meaning main-screen SafeArea is CarPlay-only; the Carlinkit dongle does not give Android Auto view-area/safe-area via HU_* files.
claims.quote: Bare `HU_SAFEAREA_INFO` is read only by `AppleCarPlay` ⇒ **main-screen SafeArea is CarPlay-only**; the Carlinkit dongle does **not** give Android Auto view-area/safe-area via `HU_*` files

claims.statement: Cluster HU_NAVISCREEN_* is a separate shared path; a genuine AA SafeArea needs a host speaking AAP directly (e.g. LIVI's own stack), not the dongle.
claims.quote: Cluster `HU_NAVISCREEN_*` is a separate shared path. A genuine AA SafeArea needs a host speaking AAP directly (e.g. LIVI's own stack), not the dongle.
